---
output:
  html_document: default
  pdf_document: default
---
## Stoppage Time — counterfactual model review

**What this is.** This is a counterfactual estimate over 314
already-played matches: *if the stoppage minutes that were truly owed but never played had
actually been played, in how many of those matches would at least one additional goal have
been score? Within this subset, how many results would have flipped (win/draw/loss)* 

This document is meant to be read start to finish with no outside material. Standard
probability and bootstrap machinery is assumed; every football- or project-specific term is
defined where it first appears. There are 7 sections. **Sections 2 and 4 are the parts I most want
scrutinized** — 2 is where the data's ambiguity is resolved by calibration, and 4 is where I explain the assumptions.

---

## 1. The claim

**In plain English.** Hold each match exactly as it was actually played. Then *append* the
stoppage minutes the referee should have added but didn't, and ask whether at least one more
goal would plausibly have landed in those appended minutes. Averaged over all 314 matches, that
probability is **X% = 23.6%**:

*If stoppage time were measured and awarded in full, 23.6% of the 314 matches would have
finished with a different scoreline* — at least one extra goal somewhere in the omitted added
time. This is a mean of per-match probabilities, computed in closed form.

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| **Headline — different scoreline (central)** | **23.6%** |
| 95% bootstrap confidence interval (sampling) | **[20.6%, 27.4%]** |
| Specification band — one modeling choice varied at a time | **21.1% – 26.1%** |
| Full envelope — all modeling choices varied jointly | **18.6% – 27.3%** |
| **Different *result*** — winner/draw actually changes | **12.1% [10.6%, 14.2%]** |
| Second-half-only variant (comparison, not the headline) | 16.0% [14.0%, 18.5%] |

*All figures are for the full match (first- and second-half stoppage combined), pooled across
all 314 matches. The two bands are different objects and are kept apart throughout: the
bootstrap CI is **sampling** uncertainty; the specification band/envelope is how X% moves as
**modeling choices** change (§5).*

**"Different scoreline" vs "different result" — keep these apart.** "≥1 extra goal anywhere" (a
different *scoreline*, 23.6%) is strictly weaker than "the winner or the draw actually changes"
(a different *result*, 12.1%). Of the 314 matches, 95 were decided by two goals or more at the
90-minute mark; we treat those as unflippable, so they can change scoreline but not result.
Conflating the two would overstate the claim, so the two numbers are always reported separately.

**The objection this most often draws — and the answer (read first).** The single most common
pushback is: *"if you added the missing minutes, teams would be less productive per minute — you
can't price new time at the urgent end-of-game rate."* I agree in principle, and the model is built
around that agreement: omitted minutes are **not** priced at the observed second-half-stoppage rate.
Instead, each marginal omitted second-half minute's scoring rate **decays** from that rate (0.0816
goals/live-min) back toward the open-play floor (0.0427) as the hypothetical added window grows —
the more time we hypothetically add, the less productive each further minute is assumed to be. The
decay half-life is an explicit, swept knob (`h ∈ [2, 8]` min, central `h = 4`), and it moves the
headline only between **22.2% and 24.9%**. The floor is the open-play rate, so even maximal decay
never prices added minutes below match-average pace — they are still football. This is the subject of
**§4.1**, which is therefore one of the two sections (with §2) most worth your scrutiny.

---

## 2. The data, its gaps, and how we resolve them

*This is the section where I'm probably most curious for your input. The model's single largest judgment call lives
here: the source data has irreducibly ambiguous gaps, and the way we resolve them is to calibrate
against one external dataset (Nate Silver, World Cup 2018) and then extrapolate to five other tournaments. The
question I am really asking is whether that calibrate-then-extrapolate move is sound.*

**The matches.** 314 matches from six major international tournaments:

| Tournament | Era | Matches |
|---|---|---|
| FIFA World Cup 2018 | PRE | 64 |
| UEFA Euro 2020 | PRE | 51 |
| FIFA World Cup 2022 | POST | 64 |
| UEFA Euro 2024 | POST | 51 |
| Copa América 2024 | POST | 32 |
| Africa Cup of Nations 2023 | POST | 52 |
| **Total** | | **314** (PRE 115, POST 199) |

A note on PRE vs POST: A 2022 IFAB directive (detail in §5) splits these into a "before" (PRE, 115) and "after" (POST,
199) cohort by *how much added time officials were instructed to show*. Basically, the instructions for the refs changed, and as a result, they added more stoppage time. Interestingly, goals per live-minute remained statistically indistinguishable across 
the split (§5), so the model **pools all six tournaments** for its scoring rates. The PRE/POST
split matters only as a robustness axis — it is not the headline, and not what I need reviewed.

**Where the numbers come from: StatsBomb event data.** Every figure traces to StatsBomb's open
event data, which is **hand-coded**: an analyst watches the match video and logs every on-ball
event — pass, shot, tackle, throw-in, foul, card, substitution — timestamped to the second.
During active play that is an event every few seconds. When the ball is dead — injury, goal
celebration, substitution, VAR review — nothing is logged, so a **gap** opens in the timestamp
stream. We use only events, scores, and timestamps: no expected-goals (xG) model, no
player-tracking ("360") data.

Because the data is event-driven, dead time is visible *only* as one of these gaps — and the gaps
are not all equal:

- **Unambiguous gaps** carry an explicit out-of-play marker on the last logged touch — the ball
  is flagged *out*, or a throw-in / goal kick / corner / free kick follows. We know the clock
  should have stopped, and roughly for how long.
- **Data Gaps** are stretches of ≥ 20 seconds with no events *and no out-of-play marker*. From
  the data alone we cannot tell whether the ball was genuinely dead (a stoppage we *should* count)
  or simply being moved slowly — often off-camera — in open play (live time we should *not* count).

**A real Data Gap — the whole problem in one example.** Austria–France, Euro 2024, second half.
These are two consecutive logged touches, with nothing recorded in between:

| Match clock | Event type | Play pattern | Ball flagged out? | Off-camera? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 50:12 | Ball Recovery (France) | Regular Play | no | no |
| 50:38 | Pass (France) | Regular Play | no | yes |

*Label definitions, for a reader without the schema: **Ball Recovery / Pass** are StatsBomb
on-ball event types; **Regular Play** means the touch happened in open play, not from a restart;
**Ball flagged out** is StatsBomb's explicit marker that the ball had left the field of play;
**Off-camera** flags a touch the analyst coded while the broadcast had cut away from the field.*

Twenty-six seconds pass between these two touches. France has the ball at 50:12 in open play, the
ball is **not** flagged out, and the next logged touch — same possession, 26 seconds later — is
coded off-camera. Was the ball dead for those 26 seconds (an untracked knock or delay we *should*
credit as owed stoppage), or alive the whole time (France simply circulating the ball while the
camera was elsewhere)? **Nothing in the data answers this.** Gaps like it total roughly **8.4
minutes per match** — far too much to credit or discard wholesale.

**How we resolve the Data Gaps.** The method is the following:

1. **Credit only marker-confirmed gaps.** A Data Gap is credited as dead *only* when its leading
   edge carries an out-of-play marker — i.e. the data independently confirms the ball had already
   left play. The ~three-quarters of Data Gaps that are *unmarked* (like the example above) are
   **dropped**. Crediting them all instead produces gross over-counts: the worst single match,
   Germany–Sweden 2018, balloons to 19.7 owed minutes and agreement with external ground truth
   collapses.
2. **Add back one small residual constant.** Dropping every unmarked gap *under*-counts. So we add
   a single flat per-match residual (24.2 s, identical for every match), fit once so the
   estimator's *mean* lands on the external ground truth for 2018 (next paragraph). It is a
   constant, not a per-gap guess.

**Does it work? Calibration against Nate Silver's hand-measured 2018 data.** Our one independent
ground truth is Nate Silver's stopwatch measurement of all 32 World Cup 2018 matches (then at
FiveThirtyEight), reporting per match the stoppage that *should* have been added ("expected") and
the stoppage that *was* played ("actual"). Building the owed-stoppage estimator one layer at a
time and scoring each against his "expected" column:

| Owed-stoppage estimator | Correlation r | MAE (min) | mean (min) |
|---|---|---|---|
| identifiable dead time only (celebration / sub / card / injury) | 0.655 | 5.72 | 7.53 |
| + excess over a standard restart allowance | 0.754 | 4.28 | 9.03 |
| + marker-confirmed Data Gaps | 0.825 | 2.49 | 12.76 |
| + residual constant (final estimator) | **0.825** | **2.44** | 12.60 |

The final estimator tracks Nate's hand-measured "expected" at **r = 0.825** (MAE 2.44 min) and
averages **12.60 min/match against his 13.16** — within half a minute. The cross-check on the
*other* column is what makes this credible: our independently measured stoppage-*played* clock
matches his "actual" almost exactly (**r = 0.992, MAE 0.13 min**). So the owed-minus-played gap
our pipeline produces reproduces the gap an outside observer found by hand — Nate's own numbers
show **13.16 owed vs 6.98 played, a 6.2-minute (1.9×) shortfall** (Figure 1).

![][f07]

*Figure 1. Validation against Nate Silver's independent, hand-measured World Cup 2018 data, 32
matches. Left: our owed-stoppage estimator vs his "expected" (should-have-been-added) minutes —
r = 0.825, MAE 2.44; the two labelled points are the model's hardest cases. Right: our
stoppage-played clock vs his "actual" (actually-played) minutes — r = 0.992, essentially exact.
The dashed line is perfect agreement; the near-exact right panel is what makes the left panel's
gap credible.*

**Two things to weigh — this is the crux.** *(i) Treating the calibrated level as a point.* If the
Data Gap credit is instead swept from "credit none" to "credit all," the headline X% moves from
**10.8% to 37.3%** — a wider swing than every other modeling choice combined. We argue that
"none" and "all" are *known-biased bounds*, not honest uncertainty, so we report the calibrated
middle as a single number and propagate only its calibration error (the MAE) through the
confidence interval (§5). That is a defensible position, but it is the one decision the headline's
tightness rests on. *(ii) Calibrate-then-extrapolate.* The entire calibration above is fit on
**World Cup 2018 only** — the lone tournament with independent ground truth — and the fitted
constants (restart allowances, residual, marker rule) are then applied **unchanged** to the other
five tournaments, where estimated owed time runs ~1.5–2× higher (POST owed ≈ 19.4 min vs 2018's
12.6). Whether a 2018-calibrated structure legitimately transfers to Copa América, AFCON, and the
rest is, in my judgment, the model's most exposed assumption — and the single thing I would most
value your read on.

**The perturbation, sized.** With the owed-stoppage estimator in hand, for each match we have the
stoppage *owed* (`T_true`), the stoppage *actually played* (`T_play`, whistle-to-whistle added
time, read off the event clock almost exactly), and their positive difference — the *omitted*
minutes `O` we inject counterfactually:

| Per match (minutes) | All 314 |
|---|---|
| Stoppage owed, `T_true` | 17.3 |
| Stoppage actually played, `T_play` | 8.9 |
| **Omitted (owed − played), `O`** | **8.6** |
| of which *live* ball-in-play (after §4) | 5.1 |

Omitted time is **positive in 97% of matches** — not a marginal perturbation. By tournament,
estimated owed time is Copa América 25.3, AFCON 23.5, World Cup 2022 17.5, Euro 2020 16.3, Euro
2024 15.6, World Cup 2018 12.6 min — the ~2× spread over the 2018 anchor that drives the
extrapolation worry above. One further thin spot to flag for later: the first-half stoppage goal
rate, a model input, rests on just **23 goals** (§5).

---

## 3. Estimand & identification

**The counterfactual, formally.** For match `i ∈ {1..314}` and half-window `h ∈ {1H, 2H}` (1H =
first half, 2H = second half):

```
T_true(i,h)   estimated true stoppage owed, in minutes (the estimator of §4.2)
T_play(i,h)   stoppage actually played = (period_end − 45:00 or 90:00), in minutes (measured)
O(i,h)        = max(0, T_true − T_play)        omitted (owed-but-unplayed) minutes
s(i,h)        = ball-in-play fraction of the window   ("live share"; measured, §4.1)
ℓ(i,h)        = O(i,h) · φ(s)                  omitted LIVE minutes
                  φ(s) = s                       (base model)
                  φ(s) = s · (1 + z(1−s))        (with the gross-up of §4.3)

λ_1H   = 0.0478   goals per live-minute, either team, in 1st-half stoppage   (n = 23 goals)
λ̄_2H(i)         = the 2nd-half rate after decay (§4.1): ramps from 0.0816 toward 0.0427

μ(i)   = λ_1H · ℓ(i,1H) + λ̄_2H(i) · ℓ(i,2H)   expected extra goals (either team)
P_change(i) = 1 − exp(−μ(i))                   probability of ≥1 extra goal (Poisson)
X%     = (1/314) · Σ_i P_change(i)
```

The metric is **deterministic**; a random seed
enters only the confidence-interval bootstrap (§5). λ is the **two-team** rate (goals by *either*
side per live-minute), so `μ = λ·ℓ` is directly the expected *count* of extra goals. (An earlier
version used a per-team rate and had to multiply by two to recover the match rate; using the
two-team rate from the start removes that factor-of-2 ambiguity.)

**Outcome-flip** replaces `P_change(i)` with `Q(i)`, keyed on the score state at 90 minutes:
level → `1 − exp(−μ)` (any goal breaks the tie); one-goal lead → `1 − exp(−μ/2)` (only the
trailing team flips it, at half the two-team rate); two-goal-or-more lead → 0. State counts at
90′: level 98, one-goal lead 121, two-or-more 95 — so 219 of 314 are flip-eligible.

**What is observed vs imputed.**

| Component | Status | How obtained |
|---|---|---|
| Goals, scoreline, score state at 45′/90′ | **Observed** | StatsBomb events |
| `T_play` stoppage actually played | **Observed** (near-exact) | whistle-to-whistle from the event clock; r = 0.992 vs Nate's "actual" (§6) |
| Ball-in-play / live share `s` | **Observed**, calibrated | gap method (§4.1); pooled World Cup 2022 within 24s of Opta's published figure |
| λ (stoppage & open-play goal rates) | **Observed** | goals ÷ live-minutes, directly counted |
| `T_true` stoppage owed | **Imputed** | the estimator of §4.2, calibrated to Nate's "expected" (r = 0.825) |
| Extra goals in omitted minutes | **Imputed** | Poisson with λ̄ applied to ℓ |

**Why there is no train/test split, and what stands in for out-of-sample validity.** The
estimand is a descriptive functional of a *fixed, fully-observed* population — all 314 matches,
not a sample drawn from a super-population we forecast into. There is no held-out outcome to
predict and no future to backtest, so cross-validation is undefined. Validity rests instead on
(a) the **inputs being unbiased** and (b) the **transfer assumptions being defensible**. The
only genuine out-of-sample anchoring is external, and it is on the *inputs*, not the output:

- the **owed-stoppage estimator** is calibrated to Nate Silver's hand-measured World Cup 2018
  "expected" (should-be-added) column — r = 0.825, mean absolute error 2.44 min, n = 32 matches;
- the **ball-in-play / live share** is calibrated to Opta's published World Cup 2022 figure of
  58:04 of ball-in-play per match (we reconstruct 57:40, 24s lower than the benchmark);
- the **stoppage-played clock** matches Nate's "actual" (actually-played) column almost exactly
  — r = 0.992, MAE 0.13 min.

There is **no external check on X% itself** — see §6 and §7.

**Identifying assumptions, and where each breaks.**

| # | Assumption | Breaks if… |
|---|---|---|
| A1 | **No re-simulation.** Appending omitted minutes does not change the minutes actually played; each extra goal is an independent increment toward "≥1 extra goal." | …game state matters — a first-half or early extra goal would change subsequent play. Internally consistent for the *scoreline* metric (we only need ≥1), but "ended differently" is not a full re-play. |
| A2 | **Productivity transfer.** Omitted minutes score at λ̄ — the observed in-stoppage rate, decayed toward the open-play floor. | …omitted minutes are systematically *less* productive than even open play (they are, after all, the minutes a referee declined to play). The floor is the open-play rate, so even maximal decay never goes below match-average pace. |
| A3 | **True-stoppage identification.** `T_true` = identifiable dead time + marker-gated Data Gaps + a small residual, pinned by calibration to Nate's "expected" (§2). | …POST behaves unlike World Cup 2018. Calibration exists for World Cup 2018 only; POST owed time (~19.4 min) is a ~1.5× extrapolation of the 2018 anchor (12.6), with all constants frozen on 2018. |
| A4 | **Rate exchangeability.** Goals-per-live-minute is the same PRE/POST; omitted minutes draw the pooled in-stoppage rate. | …the rule change altered scoring pace *per live minute* (it changed the board, not the football). The PRE/POST sweep brackets this — 22.6% vs 26.1%, within Poisson noise. |
| A5 | **Poisson within window.** Extra goals ~ Poisson(μ); `P(≥1) = 1 − e^(−μ)`. | …goals over-disperse/cluster. But `P(≥1)` depends only on the pre-first-goal hazard, so mild clustering is second-order; the observed rate already embeds some second-goal inflation (8 of 73 second-half-stoppage goals are a 2nd+ goal in the same window). |
| A6 | **Live-share neutrality.** In the base model `s` cancels in μ (it scales λ up and ℓ down equally), so μ ≈ goals-per-*clock*-min × omitted-*clock*-min. | …only through the gross-up — the one place `s` enters non-trivially. Off → geometric moves X% by ≤3 pts (§4.3). |

---

## 4. The load-bearing assumptions

This is the part worth the most scrutiny, alongside §2. Three assumptions carry the result below;
a fourth — how the ambiguous Data Gaps are resolved — is in §2. Each is stated with its rationale
and the conditions under which it breaks.

### 4.1 Productivity transfer and the late-game decay

**Takeaway.** This is the model's answer to the most common objection (flagged in §1): *added
minutes should be less productive than the urgent end-of-game minutes we observe.* Every omitted
minute must be assigned a goal rate. Late-game minutes are observably more productive than average —
but we do *not* assume the newly-added minutes inherit the full late-game premium. Their rate
**decays** from the observed in-stoppage rate back toward the open-play rate as the hypothetical
added window grows. The speed of that decay (its half-life) is the reported productivity band, and
it is the explicit knob that prices the objection in.

First, the motivation. Goals do not arrive at a constant rate through a match: the per-live-minute
scoring rate climbs through the regulation buckets, and the two stoppage windows are higher still
(Figure 2).

![][f01]

*Figure 2. Goal productivity (goals per live-minute, either team) pooled across all six
tournaments. Blue bars are regulation buckets; the two red bars are the rates inside
first- and second-half stoppage — the windows the model actually prices. Second-half
stoppage (0.082) is the highest rate anywhere — ~1.9× the open-play average. This is why omitted
stoppage minutes cannot be priced at the match-average rate; but they also cannot simply inherit
the stoppage peak, which is measured over short, unusually urgent windows.*

The three rates that anchor the model (pooled, either-team, per live-minute):

| Window | λ (goals/live-min) | 95% Poisson CI | goals (n) | live-min |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2nd-half stoppage (decay **start**) | **0.0816** | [0.0640, 0.1026] | 73 | 894.5 |
| 1st-half stoppage (used as-is, no decay) | 0.0478 | [0.0303, 0.0717] | 23 | 481.2 |
| Regulation open play (decay **floor**) | **0.0427** | [0.0396, 0.0461] | 675 | 15794.1 |

The 2nd-half stoppage rate is **1.91×** the open-play floor — but it is measured over short,
late, high-urgency windows, so we do not assume it for *all* newly-added minutes. For each
marginal omitted 2nd-half minute `t`, the rate decays geometrically from the observed rate
toward the floor:

```
λ(t)    = floor + (obs − floor) · 0.5^(t / h)
λ̄(T,h)  = floor + (obs − floor) · (1 − e^(−kT)) / (kT),   k = ln 2 / h
```

where `λ̄(T,h)` is the closed-form **average** over a window of `T` omitted 2nd-half minutes and
`h` is the half-life. The two natural bounding cases fall out exactly: `h → ∞` is "no decay"
(every omitted minute at the observed rate) and `h → 0` is "full decay" (every omitted minute at
the floor). The **first half keeps its observed rate** — decay is applied to the second half
only, where the urgency premium is concentrated. Figure 3 shows the decay curves and the rate an
average match actually receives.

![][f06]

*Figure 3. Left: the per-minute rate λ(t) for an omitted 2nd-half minute, decaying from the
observed 2nd-half-stoppage rate (0.0816, dashed) toward the open-play floor (0.0427, dotted) for
half-lives h = 2, 4 (central), 8 minutes; the grey histogram is the distribution of
omitted-minute counts across matches. Right: the window-average rate λ̄ a match actually receives
as a function of its total omitted 2nd-half minutes T; the green line marks the mean omitted
window (T ≈ 6.1 min). Because most matches have modest omitted windows, the effective rate sits
well above the floor at the central half-life.*

Reported decay band (`h ∈ [2, 8]` min, central `h = 4`), with the two limits shown for reference:

| Full match | h=2 | **h=4 (central)** | h=8 | h→∞ (no decay) | h→0 (full decay) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| X% | 22.2% | **23.6%** | 24.9% | 23.8% | 17.0% |

(`h → 0` gives 17.0% on the full match — not the 9.7% of the second-half-only variant — because
the decay floors only the 2nd-half window; the first-half contribution is untouched.)

**Where it breaks.** If late-game urgency really does persist into the newly-added minutes, the
model *under*-counts and the truth is nearer 23.8–24.9%. If the omitted minutes are *deader* than
open play — plausible, since they are precisely the minutes a referee declined to play — even the
floor overstates them; but the live-share term `ℓ` already strips out dead time, and the gross-up
(§4.3) pushes in the opposite direction. The decay half-life is the explicit knob that brackets
this assumption.

### 4.2 The owed-stoppage estimator (formal definition)

**Takeaway.** This is the formal construction of `T_true`, whose data-quality rationale and
calibration to Nate already appear in §2. It is built bottom-up from dead time we can identify
event-by-event, plus the marker-gated Data Gap allowance and residual constant of §2:

```
identifiable = (celebration ∪ substitution ∪ card ∪ injury ∪ excess-restart) ∩ dead-time
T_true       = identifiable + marker-gated Data Gaps + a small residual constant
```

`excess-restart` credits only the *excess* of a routine restart's dead time over a standard
allowance (throw-in 20s, goal kick 30s, corner 45s, free kick 60s), so ordinary restarts are not
double-paid. `marker-gated Data Gaps` credits a Data Gap (§2) of ≥20s **only when its leading edge
carries an out-of-play marker**; the ~8.4 min/match of *unmarked* Data Gaps are dropped and a flat
residual constant restores the mean. The term-by-term build and its calibration to Nate's
"expected" column (r = 0.825, MAE 2.44 min) are in §2.

**Where it breaks.** Only about a quarter of Data Gaps carry the out-of-play marker, and the
source never records "addability" directly, so r ≈ 0.82 is effectively the ceiling achievable
from free data. More structurally, the restart allowances and the residual constant are frozen on
**World Cup 2018** and applied unchanged where owed time is ~1.5–2× as large — the model's most
exposed assumption (§2, §7).

### 4.3 The gross-up: stoppage within stoppage

**Takeaway.** If you add stoppage minutes, some of *those* minutes will themselves be eaten by
new stoppages, which the directive's own logic says should also be compensated. The gross-up
adds that second-order time — but only the genuinely-dead-and-addable fraction `z` of added time
recurs, not the whole non-live share, so the effect is small and is **not** double-counting.

`z` is measured directly: of 44.2 dead minutes per match in regulation, the estimator counts 16.9
as genuine addable stoppage, so `z = 16.9 / 44.2 = 0.382`. The one-pass live factor is
`φ(s) = s·(1 + z(1−s))`; iterating stoppage-within-stoppage to a fixed point gives the geometric
`s / (1 − z(1−s))`. Because the recurring ratio is `z(1−s) ≈ 0.18` — not the naive non-live share
`1−s ≈ 0.46` — the geometric tail collapses to just above the single pass:

| Full match | gross-up OFF | **ON (central)** | geometric ceiling |
|---|---|---|---|
| X% | 21.1% | **23.6%** | 24.2% |

**Where it breaks.** If you reject second-order compensation entirely, X% falls only to **21.1%**
(still about 1 in 5). If you believed *all* dead time recurs (`z = 1`), the ceiling would be
`1/s ≈ 34%` — the `z`-weighting is exactly what kills that tail. Either way, the whole gross-up
question moves the headline by ≤ 3 points.

(The model's fourth load-bearing judgment — how the ambiguous Data Gaps are resolved, and the
calibration that pins them to Nate — is in §2, the section flagged for review. Its propagated
uncertainty, the estimator's calibration error, enters the bootstrap as a per-match term
σ = MAE·√(π/2) ≈ 3.06 min under a half-normal; see §5.)

---

## 5. Uncertainty — sampling vs specification

**Takeaway.** Two distinct kinds of uncertainty are kept separate. **(a) Sampling**: a bootstrap
CI reflecting finite goal counts and estimator error. **(b) Specification**: how X% moves as
modeling choices change. Once the Data Gap handling is fixed (§2), the specification spread is
comparable to the sampling spread — neither dominates.

**(a) Sampling CI = [20.6%, 27.4%]** (width 6.7 pts). In each of B = 1000 bootstrap draws (seed
fixed): every distinct λ-cell is redrawn from its Jeffreys–Gamma posterior
`Gamma(count + 0.5, 1/exposure)` — the 2nd-half decay redraws *both* the 73-goal observed cell
and the 675-goal floor cell and recombines them through λ̄ — plus one shared owed-stoppage
estimator-error draw `N(0, 3.06 min)`, split across the two halves by their share of addable time
and re-shifting the decay horizon. This is genuine sampling uncertainty in the inputs.

**(b) Specification sensitivities** (each choice swept with the others held at central, full
match):

| Modeling choice | Levels → X% | Spread |
|---|---|---|
| **λ source** | all-pooled **23.6** · POST-only 22.6 · regime-matched 23.8 · PRE-only 26.1 | ~3.5 pts |
| **Gross-up** | off 21.1 → **on 23.6** → geometric 24.2 | ~3.1 pts |
| **Decay half-life** | h=2 22.2 · **h=4 23.6** · h=8 24.9 | ~2.7 pts |
| **Conditioning** | overall **23.6** · split by tied/not-tied 23.4 | ~0.2 pts (negligible) |
| One-at-a-time band (min–max of the above) | **21.1% – 26.1%** | 5.0 pts ≈ 0.7× sampling |
| Full **joint** envelope (all choices together) | **18.6% – 27.3%** | 8.7 pts ≈ 1.3× sampling |
| *Data Gap handling, if (improperly) swept as a knob* | *10.8% – 37.3%* | *~26 pts — see §2* |

The **λ source** row is where the PRE/POST split (§2) earns its keep. The 2022 directive raised
stoppage *played* sharply — 6.8 → 10.0 min/match, PRE → POST — yet goals *per live-minute* barely
moved: pricing omitted minutes at PRE-only, POST-only, or pooled rates spans just 22.6–26.1%, all
within Poisson noise. Stoppage time changed; productivity did not. That is precisely why the model
pools all six tournaments for λ ("all-pooled" above), and why the top-end 26.1% comes entirely
from the thin, wide-CI PRE-only cohort rather than any real PRE/POST productivity gap.
("regime-matched" prices PRE omitted minutes at the PRE rate and POST minutes at the POST rate,
rather than pooling.) 

**Which choices move X% most.** The **λ source** (driven entirely by the thin, wide-CI PRE-only
cohort at the top end) and the **gross-up** are the widest legitimate axes, ~3 points each; the
**decay half-life** is ~2.7; conditioning is noise. Both extremes of the reported envelope are
identifiable: the floor (18.6–21.1%) is the gross-up-off / most-decay corner; the ceiling
(26.1–27.3%) is the PRE-only λ × least-decay corner — i.e. the least-certain input cohort. (The
envelope sweeps the half-life over its plausible 2–8 min range; the two mathematical limits — no
decay, full decay — are shown in §4.1 only to recover the bounding rails and are not part of the
band.)

**A structural soft spot.** The first-half window contributes **+7.6 points** (second-half-only
16.0% → full-match 23.6%) — roughly a third of the headline — and rests on the **23-goal**
first-half-stoppage rate. The bootstrap propagates that thinness (a wide first-half CI), but the
*point* estimate's dependence on a 23-goal cell is worth naming.

---

## 6. External validation

**Takeaway.** Two external anchors certify the model's **inputs**; neither certifies its
**output**.

- **Nate Silver, World Cup 2018 (Figure 1, §2).** Detailed in §2: our owed-stoppage estimator
  tracks his hand-measured "expected" at **r = 0.825** (MAE 2.44 min) and our stoppage-played
  clock tracks his "actual" at **r = 0.992**. The point worth repeating here is that the
  owed-vs-played *gap* is not a modeling artifact — Nate, with a stopwatch and no access to our
  pipeline, independently found **13.16 owed vs 6.98 played**, i.e. roughly half the owed stoppage
  going unplayed.
- **Opta, World Cup 2022 ball-in-play.** Opta publishes an official average ball-in-play time of
  58:04 per match for World Cup 2022; our gap-method reconstruction gives 57:40 (24s low). This
  certifies the live-share denominator `s`.

**What they do *not* certify.** Both anchors are World Cup 2018/2022 respectively; the POST owed-time
magnitude (~19.4 min) is an extrapolation. 

## 7. Limitations & open questions

Honest list, least-defensible first:

1. **No independent counterfactual benchmark.** Every external check is on an input; X% itself is
   unfalsified against any outside number. 
2. **POST coverage gap.** The estimator is calibrated on World Cup 2018 only. The POST cohort —
   where the headline mostly lives, and where owed time is ~1.5–2× World Cup 2018 — rides on
   frozen-2018 constants and a single World Cup 2022 ball-in-play point. If the 2018
   marker/allowance/residual structure does not transfer to Copa América and AFCON, the POST
   `T_true` (and thus X%) is the most exposed quantity.
3. **Data-Gap-as-point (§2).** Defensible, but it is the choice that makes the band tight; a
   reviewer who insists "none"/"all" are legitimate uncertainty will see a far wider honest range.
4. **First-half thinness (§5).** About a third of the headline rests on 23 goals, with no
   game-state propagation across the first-half increment (A1).
5. **Productivity transfer is an assumption, not a measurement (§4.1).** The decay band brackets
   the urgency premium but cannot rule out that *omitted* minutes — the ones a referee chose not
   to play — are systematically unlike any observed minute.
6. **Cooling/water breaks** were investigated as an additional stoppage source and set aside:
   roughly three-quarters of that time is already captured by the estimator, and crediting the
   rest added no robust accuracy on the only externally-validated set.
